content: The capacitive reactance of a capacitor connected to 50 V d.c. is

An air capacitor of plate area ' $A$ ' and separation between the plates is ' $d$ ' has a capacity ' $C$ '. Two dielectric slabs are inserted between its plates in two different manners as shown in figure. The capacitance of a capacitor is
explanation:
When dielectric slabs of thickness $t_1$ and $t_2$, and constants $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$, are inserted in series between the plates (as shown), we treat the configuration like a series of three capacitors: dielectric 1 , dielectric 2 , and air.

Total effective distance:

$$ d_{e f f}=\frac{t_1}{k_1}+\frac{t_1}{k_1}+\left(d-t_1-t_2\right) $$

Capacitance is:

$$ C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_{e f f}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-t_1-t_2+\frac{t_1}{k_1}+\frac{t_2}{k_2}} $$

content: When three capacitors of capacitances ' C ', ' 2 C '? and ' 3 C ' are connected in series. The potential difference applied across the combination is ' $V$ '. The energy stored in the circuit is
explanation:
Since the capacitors $\mathrm{C}, 2 \mathrm{C}$ and 3 C are connected in series, their equivalent resistance will be:

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{C_{e q}} & =\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{2 C}+\frac{1}{3 C}=\frac{11}{6 C} \\ \Rightarrow \quad C_{e q} & =\frac{6 C}{11} \end{aligned} $$

Energy stored in the circuit,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}} \mathrm{~V}^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6 \mathrm{C}}{11} \times \mathrm{V}^2 \\ & =\frac{3}{11} \mathrm{CV}^2 \end{aligned} $$

content: In between the plates of parallel plate capacitor of plate separation 'd' a dielectric plate of thickness ' $t$ ' is inserted. The capacitance becomes one-third of the original capacity without dielectric. The dielectric constant of the plate is
explanation:
Without any dielectric slab, the capacitor’s capacitance is given by $C=\frac{A \varepsilon_0}{d}$

When the dielectric slab of thickness t is inserted, the capacitor behaves like two capacitors in series (one of air thickness d−t and one of dielectric thickness t). The new capacitance is $$\mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{A} \varepsilon_0}{\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{k}}}$$

We are told that this new capacitance is one-third of the original, i.e. $$\mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{3} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\mathrm{~A} \varepsilon_0}{\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{k}}} =\frac{1}{3}\,\frac{\mathrm{~A} \varepsilon_0}{\mathrm{~d}}$$

Canceling common factors and solving for k yields $$k=\frac{t}{2\,d+t}$$

content: A parallel combination of two capacitors of capacities ' C ' and ' $\frac{\mathrm{C}}{3}$, respectively is connected across a battery of 12 volt. When both capacitors are fully charged, the charge and energy stored in them is $Q_1, Q_2$ and $E_1, E_2$ respectively. Then the ratio of $\left(E_1-E_2\right)$ to $\left(Q_1-Q_2\right)$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} \quad & Q=C V \\\therefore & Q_1=12 C \text { and } Q_2=4 C \\ & \text { Also, } E=\frac{1}{2} C V^2 \\ \therefore \quad & E_1=\frac{1}{2} C_1 V^2=\frac{1}{2} \times C \times 144 \\  & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =72 C \\ & \text { Similarly, } E_2=\frac{1}{2} C_2 V^2=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{C}{3} \times 144 \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=24 C \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{E_1-E_2}{Q_1-Q_2}=\frac{48 C}{8 C}=\frac{6}{1} \end{array} $$
